text: Transverse perineal support operation for perineal descent Video vignette We present the case of a fifty-five years old female referred to our unit for obstructive defecation symptoms with an ODS score of twenty-two. Clinical evaluation revealed a weak perineal body with a four centimeter descending perineum during straining attempt. Proctoscopy performed in clinic was positive for occult rectal prolapse. Colonoscopy was negative.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy under spinal anesthesia for the surgical procedure. The surgeon performs an internal Delorme procedure, addressing the diagnosed rectal prolapse and descending perineum.

The patient is prepped and draped in a sterile fashion. The surgeon evaluates perineal weakness digitally before performing bilateral perineal incisions at the ischial tuberosity. Dissection is carried out anteriorly to the transverse perineal muscle using a blunt trochar, and an O-proline suture is passed through. A tailored biological mesh is positioned and fixed to aponeurotic bony structures with two O-PDS sutures.

The surgeon closes the skin at the perineal incision sites using trio-vicryl rapid interrupted sutures. Digital examination confirms the resistance and strength of the perineal area. Mild hematoma is visible at the incision sites.

Patient was discharged on the fourth post-operative day. Follow-up was scheduled at six months and one year. A digital evaluation of perineum strengthness and a dynamic evaluation during straining was performed. We didn't record any mesh-related complications. Patient didn't report any sexual or voiding dysfunctions, while obstructive symptoms were still improved at one-year follow-up. ODS score was eight and eleven, respectively, at six months and one year.
